text: David counted the troops with him and appointed commanders over units of a thousand and a hundred.
He sent the army out in three groups — one under Joab, one under Joab's brother Abishai, and one under Ittai the Gittite — and told them he intended to go out with them himself.

But the troops objected, saying he should not go; if they had to retreat, no one would care, and even if half of them died it wouldn't matter as much, but he alone was worth ten thousand of them, and it would be better for him to be ready to send help from the city.
The king agreed to do whatever seemed best to them, and stood beside the gate while the whole army marched out by hundreds and thousands.
In front of everyone, David instructed Joab, Abishai, and Ittai to deal gently, for his sake, with the young man Absalom, and the whole army heard him give that order.
So the troops marched out into the field to meet Israel, and the battle took place in the forest of Ephraim.
Israel's forces were defeated there by David's men, and the losses that day were enormous — twenty thousand men.
The fighting spread out across the whole region, and the forest itself claimed more lives that day than the actual battle did.

Absalom happened to run into some of David's men; riding his mule, he passed beneath the thick branches of a large oak, and his head got caught fast in the tree — he was left hanging between heaven and earth while the mule kept going without him.
A man who saw this told Joab that he had seen Absalom hanging in an oak tree.
Joab asked why, if he had seen him, he hadn't struck him down right there — he would have given him ten pieces of silver and a warrior's belt for it.

The man replied that even for a thousand pieces of silver he would not have raised a hand against the king's son, since they had all heard the king order Joab, Abishai, and Ittai to protect the young man Absalom for his sake.
He added that if he had betrayed Absalom, David would eventually have found out — nothing stays hidden from the king — and Joab himself would have stood back and let him take the blame.
Joab said he wasn't going to waste any more time on this, took three javelins, and drove them into Absalom's chest while he was still alive in the tree.
Ten of Joab's young armor-bearers then surrounded Absalom, struck him, and finished killing him.
Joab then sounded the trumpet, calling the troops back from chasing the rest of Israel's army, and they stopped pursuing.
They took Absalom's body, threw it into a large pit in the forest, and piled a great heap of stones over it, while the rest of Israel's men fled home.

Absalom had, while he was still alive, set up a stone pillar for himself in the King's Valley, reasoning that he had no son to keep his name alive; he named it after himself, and it was still known as Absalom's monument.
Ahimaaz, Zadok's son, asked Joab to let him run and tell the king that the LORD had rescued him from his enemies.
Joab told him not to be the one to carry the news that day — he could carry news another time, but not today, because the king's son was dead.
Instead, Joab told a Cushite man to go report to the king what he had seen, and the Cushite bowed to Joab and ran off.
Ahimaaz asked Joab again, whatever happens, let me run too, after the Cushite; Joab asked why he wanted to, since he wouldn't get any reward for this news.
Ahimaaz insisted he wanted to run regardless, so Joab let him go, and he ran by way of the plain and got ahead of the Cushite.
David was sitting between the two gates when a watchman climbed to the roof over the gate by the wall, looked out, and saw a man running alone.
The watchman called down to tell the king, who said that if the man was alone he was probably bringing news; the runner kept getting closer.
Then the watchman spotted another man running and called out to the gatekeeper that another runner was coming alone; the king said this one, too, was probably bringing news.
The watchman said the first man's running reminded him of Ahimaaz, Zadok's son, and the king said he was a good man who would come with good news.

Ahimaaz called out to the king that all was well, bowed down before him with his face to the ground, and said blessed be the LORD your God, who handed over the men who rebelled against my lord the king.
The king asked if the young man Absalom was safe, and Ahimaaz answered that when Joab sent him he saw a great commotion but didn't know exactly what had happened.
The king told him to step aside and wait, so he moved over and stood there.
Then the Cushite arrived and announced good news for the king — that the LORD had rescued him that day from everyone who had risen against him.

The king asked the Cushite the same question — was the young man Absalom safe? — and the Cushite answered that he wished every enemy of the king, and everyone who rose up against him with evil intent, would end up like that young man.

The king was overcome with grief; he went up to the room above the gate, weeping as he walked, crying out, my son Absalom, my son, my son Absalom — if only I had died in your place, Absalom, my son, my son.
